output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.3, "R1.6": 0.4, "R1.7": 0.5}}

Summary: Critique focused on a serious overreach in the conclusion and several evidence-claim gaps; the methods/metrics themselves are reported cleanly, but the inference from "calibrated on a COSMOS-derived held-out set" to "use directly for LSST 3x2pt without per-bin recalibration" is not supported by what was actually tested.

Key issues:

1. The held-out set is not independent of the training set in the way the conclusion requires. The 80/20 split is drawn from the same COSMOS2020 high-confidence-flag sample, so the "held-out" metrics measure in-distribution generalization, not transfer to the LSST gold sample. The spec-z subsample of COSMOS is known to be biased toward bluer, brighter, emission-line-rich galaxies at fixed i-magnitude, especially at i > 23. A uniform PIT on this subsample does not imply a uniform PIT on the photometric LSST gold sample it is meant to stand in for. The final paragraph treats these as equivalent; they are not.

2. The recommendation to skip per-tomographic-bin recalibration does not follow from the evidence presented. Global PIT uniformity and global 68% coverage are compatible with substantial bin-dependent miscalibration — over-coverage in one redshift bin can cancel under-coverage in another and still yield a flat global PIT. 3x2pt analyses are sensitive to the n(z) of each tomographic bin individually, not to the global stacked n(z). To support the stated recommendation, you need PIT/coverage diagnostics computed within each tomographic bin (and ideally within magnitude bins), not just over the full sample.

3. "Calibrated across the full LSST gold-sample regime, including faint, high-redshift galaxies" is stronger than the data shown. The held-out set spans i = 17.5–25.3 and 0 < z < 3, but the metrics are reported only as aggregates. The faint/high-z corner is precisely where COSMOS spec-z completeness collapses and where calibration is most likely to fail. Either restrict the claim to the regime where the diagnostic is informative, or add stratified metrics (σ_NMAD, outlier fraction, PIT, coverage) in 2D bins of (i, z).

4. The "5σ bootstrap" claim needs more detail to be interpretable. Bootstrap p-values that small are usually artifacts of the resampling unit (per-galaxy vs. per-field) and of comparing point estimates without accounting for spec-z selection. State what was resampled, what statistic, against which baseline, and whether the test controls for the fact that BPZ/EAZY priors were not retuned on COSMOS.

5. Minor evidence gaps worth closing:
   - "50 CNNs" — identical architectures with different seeds, or architectural diversity? Ensemble diversity drives the predictive variance you are calibrating.
   - "Gaussian-mixture readout" — how many components, fit how? This determines whether the posterior can represent the bimodal degeneracies (e.g., low-z red / high-z blue) that drive catastrophic outliers.
   - "Temperature-scaling calibration" on 80% of the data — applied to what? A single scalar temperature on a mixture posterior is unusual; clarify whether it scales component variances, mixture entropy, or something else.
   - CRPS "lower than every baseline" — report the numbers, not just the ordering.

6. Prose: "underpins virtually every cosmological analysis from upcoming Stage-IV surveys" should be "for upcoming Stage-IV surveys" (the analyses are not from the surveys). "p(z|f) where f denotes the observed flux vector" — earlier you said the inputs are ugrizy photometry fed to CNNs, which usually implies images, not a flux vector; reconcile.

7. Novelty framing is absent. Deep ensembles for photo-z with mixture readouts have been explored (e.g., Pasquet+19, Schuldt+21, Jones & Singal, Treyer+24). State explicitly what DEEP-z does that those do not — the contribution as written could be read as a re-implementation.

Recommended rewrite of the final paragraph (illustrative):
"On the held-out COSMOS2020 subsample, the global PIT histogram is uniform and 68% coverage is recovered to within 0.1%. We caution that this subsample is not representative of the photometric LSST gold sample at i ≳ 23, and that global calibration does not imply per-tomographic-bin calibration. We therefore present DEEP-z as a strong candidate posterior estimator for LSST, while recommending per-bin recalibration against clustering-redshift or self-organizing-map references before use in 3x2pt cosmology."

Uncertainty disclosure: The specific COSMOS spec-z selection biases I cite (bluer/brighter/emission-line preference at faint i) are well-established qualitatively but I have not verified the exact magnitude limits against a current reference; the prior-work citations in point 7 are from memory and should be checked before inclusion.
